talk_title: Implementation gaps and early data from the TUBAWISP-2 salpingectomy trial

speaker.name: Rebecca Stone
speaker.affiliation: Johns Hopkins University, Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology

biomarkers.name: BRCA1 pathogenic variant
biomarkers.finding: 56% of BRCA1 carriers delay risk‑reducing surgery beyond age 40

biomarkers.name: BRCA2 pathogenic variant
biomarkers.finding: ≈33% of BRCA2 carriers delay risk‑reducing surgery beyond age 45

biomarkers.name: STIC presence at risk‑reducing surgery
biomarkers.finding: ≈30% 10‑year risk of peritoneal high‑grade serous cancer when STIC is present

models.name: TUBAWISP-2
models.genotype_or_key_features: high‑risk women with germline BRCA1/2 or other pathogenic variants

clinical_data.population: women with STIC at risk‑reducing surgery

clinical_data.metric: hazard ratio for serous ovarian cancer with opportunistic salpingectomy (BC, Canada)
adoption rate of opportunistic salpingectomy at inpatient hysterectomy (US claims data)
adoption rate of salpingectomy as alternative to tubal ligation (outpatient)

MOA_summary: Stone outlined the epidemiologic and health‑policy data supporting salpingectomy as a primary ovarian cancer prevention strategy, presented early enrollment data from the TUBAWISP-2 preference trial comparing standard risk‑reducing salpingo‑oophorectomy to early salpingectomy with delayed oophorectomy, and highlighted implementation barriers (coding, coverage, disparities) that limit adoption despite a 0.22 hazard ratio for serous ovarian cancer.
key_findings: 56% of BRCA1 carriers and ~33% of BRCA2 carriers delay risk‑reducing surgery beyond recommended ages, increasing lifetime serous cancer risk.
Presence of STIC at risk‑reducing surgery confers ~30% 10‑year risk of peritoneal high‑grade serous carcinoma.
Opportunistic salpingectomy reduces serous ovarian cancer risk by ~80% (HR 0.22) in the BC, Canada population‑based cohort.
US adoption of opportunistic salpingectomy at inpatient hysterectomy has plateaued at ~60% since 2018, with lower uptake in outpatient settings (11‑14%).
Racial and insurance disparities exist: Black women and Medicaid recipients are less likely to receive salpingectomy during tubal ligation or postpartum sterilization.
open_questions: How can health‑policy and reimbursement reforms accelerate universal adoption of opportunistic salpingectomy?
What are the optimal surveillance or interception strategies for women with residual STIC after salpingectomy?

talk_title: OVASEEK: hyperspectral and fluorescence imaging platform for whole fallopian tube screening

speaker.name: Angela Belcher
speaker.affiliation: Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Department of Biological Engineering

models.name: fallopian tube specimens
models.genotype_or_key_features: 86 tubes from risk‑reducing salpingectomy patients, including BRCA1/2 carriers

MOA_summary: Belcher described the OVASEEK system that combines near‑infrared hyperspectral imaging, >1000 nm fluorescent nanoparticle labeling, and machine‑learning segmentation to scan entire excised fallopian tubes in ~15 minutes, identify regions with atypical optical signatures, and predict BRCA mutation status and nanoparticle binding with >90% accuracy, enabling whole‑organ detection of STIC and pre‑cancer lesions.
readouts: optical biometrics (tube length, weight, water/lipid content)
key_findings: Imaged 86 fallopian tubes; initial prototype required 8 hours per tube, later optimized toward 15‑minute workflow.
Machine‑learning model predicts presence of gold‑nanoparticle binding sites with 86% accuracy based on hyperspectral data alone.
BRCA1/2 mutation status can be inferred from optical spectra with 90% accuracy at the whole‑tube level and 95% at the cellular level.
Detected a previously missed STIC in a colon‑surgery patient by highlighting a <200 µm atypical region for targeted pathology.
Developed a seventh‑generation instrument and custom edge‑computing chips (first batch expected June) to enable rapid on‑site analysis.
open_questions: Can OVASEEK be integrated into routine pathology labs to replace or augment standard CFEM sampling?
What additional molecular markers (e.g., metabolic NAD/FAD signatures) can be captured at the 800‑980 nm range to improve lesion classification?

cited_papers: Bhatia et al. 2023 Nature Biomedical Engineering
patent_or_ip_hints: US Patent Application for OVASEEK hyperspectral imaging system

talk_title: Orion FISH platform reveals polyclonal copy‑number heterogeneity in STIC lesions

speaker.name: Dr. Drapkin
speaker.affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Department of Pathology

models.name: STIC and adjacent normal fallopian tube epithelium

MOA_summary: Using the Orion FISH platform, Drapkin performed simultaneous 16‑plex immunofluorescence and multiplex DNA‑FISH on single tissue sections, revealing that MYC and CCNE1 amplifications in STIC lesions are polyclonal and spatially heterogeneous, with MYC‑amplified cells often adjacent to immune infiltrates, while CCNE1‑amplified regions are immune‑cold; unexpected copy‑number gains of MDM4 were also detected in morphologically normal epithelium, especially in BRCA1 carriers.
key_findings: STIC lesions display polyclonal copy‑number alterations: distinct sub‑clones harbor MYC amplification, CCNE1 amplification, or both.
MYC‑amplified regions are enriched for neighboring immune cells, whereas CCNE1‑amplified regions show reduced immune infiltration.
Copy‑number gains of MYC and CCNE1 are also observed in adjacent histologically normal tubal epithelium.
MDM4 copy‑number gain occurs in both secretory (PAX8+) and ciliated (FOXP1+) cells of normal tubes, more frequently in BRCA1 carriers.
Validation with the clinically approved BioView platform confirms the accuracy of Orion FISH copy‑number calls.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: MYC‑amplified cells appear to attract immune cells, suggesting a genotype‑dependent immune microenvironment that may influence lesion progression.
open_questions: Do specific copy‑number patterns (e.g., MYC vs. CCNE1 amplification) predict which STICs will progress to invasive disease?
Can genotype‑guided immune modulation be used to prevent progression of polyclonal STIC lesions?
